topic: Eclipse magnetic ensemble (39 eclipses) — Kim & Chang (2018)
change_summary: Verdict flipped Self-Contradicted → Std Model Explains on 2026-04-13 (per verdict_history). finding/detail_evidence/detail_verdict_text all rewritten since c3 fingerprint (2026-04-12). detail_evidence still contains a 'Self-contradiction:' paragraph that references the refraction cascade — potentially out of step with the new Std Model Explains verdict.
our_argument_summary: Verdict is 'Std Model Explains'. Finding and TLDRs argue: Chapman (1933) ionospheric Sq-current mechanism predicts the 39-eclipse ensemble signal; dome provides no derivation, only a post-hoc scaling formula; and WIN-068 triple-counts the same observational phenomenon already counted as WIN-010 (BOU 2017) and WIN-025 (2024 9-station). Detail evidence also retains a residual refraction-cascade self-contradiction paragraph that targets the dome's own stated mechanism (aetheric field perturbation by geometric shadow).

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No monitor.py snippet exists in our raw-text for this WIN; the 39-eclipse ensemble is a retrospective literature citation (Kim & Chang 2018) rather than a live automation target. The dome's 2026 eclipse predictions (E001–E009) use ΔZ = baseline × coverage × FSF with FSF derived from V12 H(r)/r geometry, but those are future-facing and not what WIN-068 asserts about 1991–2016.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: N/A for the ensemble — there is no comparison logic. The dome lists the peer-reviewed 39-eclipse meta-analysis as 'confirmed' without any code-side validation.
data_sources_used: Kim & Chang (2018) Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054 (citation as claimed)
INTERMAGNET (historical dataset used by Kim & Chang)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None visible. The WIN is asserted qualitatively; there is no numeric tolerance to breach because there is no dome-derived expected value against which Kim & Chang's observations are compared.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None detected relative to what we claim, but note that the V50.6 raw-text dump does NOT contain WIN-068 explicitly — it was added after the V51 dome bump. Our raw-text snapshot is stale for this WIN; recommend capturing a V51+ snapshot if the dome retains its current wording.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: V50.6 raw-text captures no automation for the 39-eclipse ensemble. Kim & Chang (2018) is a historical meta-analysis of data spanning 1991–2016 — there is no live API fetch, no per-eclipse comparison, and no tolerance check for this claim. The dome's monitor.py (as captured in prior reviews) has eclipse-related entries only for the 2026 future event (PRED-E001..E009), not the 39-eclipse ensemble. Tag: none.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Chapman (1933) + modern Sq-current framework (Yamazaki & Maute 2021) predict eclipse magnetic perturbations — Y-component rise, X/Z/F decrease, ~30°–50° peak, ~180-minute window — from well-understood ionospheric conductivity depletion. The dome relabels this as 'aetheric field perturbation by geometric shadow blocking the field interaction between sun and dome' (raw-text/02-wins.txt PROS-003). Same phenomenon, substituted vocabulary, no new quantitative prediction.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Kim & Chang (2018) published the 39-eclipse analysis covering 1991–2016 before the dome incorporated it. The dome restates the meta-analysis result as a 'WIN' in V51.x. No prior-to-data dome prediction document exists for this ensemble. (OTS timestamping on status_history.json would anchor the observation side regardless — see Timestamp Error tab.)
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No derivation from disc_radius, firmament_height, or any dome geometric parameter produces Kim & Chang's component pattern, latitudinal peak, or 180-minute duration. The dome asserts a mechanism in prose ('aetheric field perturbation by geometric shadow') but provides no equation linking dome geometry to the observed magnitude, spatial pattern, or timing of the 39-eclipse ensemble signal.

holes_found.description: Verdict–text incoherence: the verdict is 'Std Model Explains' but detail_evidence paragraph 2 leads with 'Self-contradiction: the dome's own refraction fix breaks this prediction' and paragraph 4 argues the dome's own mechanism requires a definite physical sun altitude that is now indeterminate. These are Self-Contradicted-grade arguments embedded inside a Std Model Explains verdict. A dome defender can legitimately say: 'Pick one — you either think Chapman explains it (Std Model Explains) or you think the refraction cascade breaks it (Self-Contradicted); the hedge is rhetorical overreach.' The 2026-04-13 verdict revert explicitly justified Std Model Explains with 'Eclipse magnetic effects track apparent shadow geometry (Chapman Sq), not physical sun position' — the cascade paragraph directly contradicts this stated rationale.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) demote the cascade argument to a short cross-reference ('For a separate internal-consistency concern targeting the dome's own mechanism, see Section 1.8') and delete paragraphs 2–4's detailed cascade reasoning, OR (b) add a single-sentence reconciliation at the top of detail_evidence explaining why we keep Std Model Explains despite the cascade finding — e.g., 'Standard ionospheric physics fully explains the observation using the apparent shadow geometry, so we do not need the refraction cascade to invalidate this WIN; the cascade note below is included only as a subsidiary finding against the dome's own stated mechanism.' Option (a) is cleaner.

holes_found.description: Cross-WIN inconsistency in cascade presentation: WIN-010 (BOU 2017) and WIN-025 (2024 9-station) both have 'Std Model Explains' verdicts and do NOT include the refraction-cascade self-contradiction paragraph. Only WIN-068 carries the cascade narrative. All three WINs describe the same physical phenomenon with the same dome mechanism; if the cascade applies at all, it applies to all three equally. Having it in only one of the three is structurally odd and invites a defender to ask 'why here and not there?'
holes_found.recommendation: Align the three eclipse-magnetic WINs: if we want the cascade mentioned at all, mention it identically (preferably via a shared cross-reference to Section 1.8) in each WIN's detail_evidence. If we want to remove it from WIN-068 (per hole 1), also ensure Section 1.8 lists all three as affected so readers can still trace the self-contradiction at the section level.

holes_found.description: Triple-counting phrasing is self-referential and slightly confusing: 'triple-counts the same phenomenon as WIN-010, WIN-025, and WIN-068'. WIN-068 IS the WIN being read; the list includes itself as one of the three. Logically fine (WIN-068 + WIN-010 + WIN-025 = three entries for one phenomenon) but phrased awkwardly as if WIN-068 is counting itself against itself.
holes_found.recommendation: Rephrase finding to: 'Dome adds no equation and this entry duplicates the observational basis already counted as WIN-010 (BOU 2017) and WIN-025 (2024 9-station ensemble) — the 39-eclipse Kim & Chang meta-analysis is a larger aggregation of the same phenomenon.'

holes_found.description: Paragraph 3 of detail_evidence ('The dome's own 2026 eclipse predictions (E001–E009) use the formula ΔZ = baseline × coverage × FSF…') is off-topic for this WIN. WIN-068 is about the historical 1991–2016 ensemble, not the 2026 predictions. Arguing that FSF-based future predictions share the same refraction-cascade weakness is a separate point that belongs in the predictions-catalog or Section 1.8 — not in the evidence for a retrospective ensemble WIN.
holes_found.recommendation: Move paragraph 3's content to Section 1.8 or the predictions catalog entry for E001–E009. Keep WIN-068's evidence block focused on the ensemble: Kim & Chang's data, the Chapman/Sq mechanism, and the cluster-duplication observation.

holes_found.description: Quantitative claim inherited without on-panel check: the cascade paragraph cites H(r) ≈ 3,618 km at Tropic of Cancer and ~1,975 km at Tropic of Capricorn with a >1,600 km seasonal swing. Those figures derive from Section 1.8's r↔latitude mapping, which is non-trivial on the dome's elliptical disc (disc_radius 20,015 km; H(r)=8537·exp(-r/8619) exactly matches those H values only at r≈7,402 km and r≈12,618 km respectively, which is not the naive (90−|lat|)/90 × disc_radius mapping). WIN-068 simply asserts the numbers. If Section 1.8's mapping is later revised, WIN-068 becomes quietly wrong without any change to its own text.
holes_found.recommendation: Either add a one-line parenthetical citation — '(see Section 1.8 for the r→latitude derivation of these H(r) values)' — or drop the specific numbers from WIN-068 entirely and keep only the qualitative claim that H(r) varies seasonally, letting Section 1.8 carry the arithmetic. Reduces coupling and repair cost.

holes_found.description: Claim header 'Eclipse magnetic ensemble (39 eclipses)' is clear, but the post-hoc framing in detail_verdict_text paragraph 4 ('Kim & Chang's published dataset covering 1991–2016, publicly available well before the dome incorporated it') does not give a specific dome-incorporation date. Without a date, 'well before' is soft. Dome defenders can push back with 'when exactly?'
holes_found.recommendation: Specify the dome version that first added WIN-068 (e.g., V51.0 in early 2026 per our verdict_history) and contrast with Kim & Chang's 2018 publication year. Nine-year gap is stronger than 'well before'.

holes_found.description: TLDR/detail drift: tldr_evidence and tldr_verdict are pure Std Model Explains framing (Chapman mechanism + triple-counting). They do not mention the refraction cascade at all. A detail-level reader encounters the self-contradiction paragraph; a TLDR-only reader does not. This is the opposite of the verdict-text mismatch c3 flagged in the prior direction, but it still invites the 'your levels disagree' attack.
holes_found.recommendation: Once hole 1 is resolved (removing or subordinating the cascade in detail_evidence), TLDRs naturally align. If hole 1 is deferred, consider adding a single sentence to tldr_evidence acknowledging the cascade as a secondary concern.

stronger_arguments.description: The Field Strength Factor (FSF) is described by the dome's own eclipse formulas as having station-specific values 0.642–2.075 across nine European stations at similar disc radii — a 3.2× spread. That is empirical fitting, not derivation. We hint at this in the current detail_verdict_text, but it can be sharpened: if FSF were dome-geometric, stations at the same r should produce the same FSF (up to small geometric differences). A 3.2× spread at similar r is direct evidence the dome is station-fitting, not predicting.
sources: Dome source for FSF station table

stronger_arguments.description: Kim & Chang's 30°–50° magnetic-latitude peak is a specific consequence of the Sq current vortex geometry in the dipole-based geomagnetic coordinate system. The dome has no analog of the Sq vortex, and its flat-disc coordinate system has no physical reason to produce a peak at that band — yet the 39-eclipse ensemble shows it. This is a 'why does this specific latitude band light up?' challenge the dome cannot meet, and it's a stronger discriminator than simple post-hoc framing.
sources: Yamazaki & Maute 2021, Front. Astron. Space Sci. (Sq currents)
Kim & Chang 2018 Fig. 4 (latitudinal dependence) — paper claim, not independently verified here

kernel_of_truth.description: Kim & Chang (2018) is real, peer-reviewed, and shows a genuine statistical signal across 39 eclipses. The dome is correct that a real, reproducible geomagnetic perturbation tracks eclipse geometry. Dismissing this as 'made up' would be a strawman. Additionally, the dome's instinct that a single observatory (WIN-010) is weaker than a multi-eclipse ensemble is methodologically sound.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The dome's correctness about the observation's existence is beside the point — the question is whether the observation discriminates dome cavity dynamics from standard ionospheric physics. Chapman (1933) + modern Sq models predict the exact same pattern from first principles. The dome's contribution is renaming the cause, not predicting a different value. A genuinely discriminating dome prediction would be a different magnitude, a different latitude band, or a different temporal profile — none of which the dome supplies.

deeper_analysis: The verdict oscillation (Std Model Explains → Self-Contradicted → Std Model Explains across two days) reveals that we haven't fully decided which is the load-bearing argument. The cleanest path forward: Std Model Explains is the primary, most defensible verdict because the standard model independently predicts the signal and therefore the WIN fails the discriminatory-power test regardless of refraction cascade. The cascade is a secondary argument against the dome's own mechanism, not a reason to elevate the verdict. Keeping the cascade as a prominent paragraph in this WIN's detail_evidence creates friction between the verdict label and the body text that a defender can exploit. Either commit fully to the subordination (fold cascade into a cross-reference) or exploit the other path and change the verdict back to Self-Contradicted — but not both. Structurally, hole 2 (cross-WIN inconsistency with WIN-010/025) is the tell: if the cascade really applies here, why doesn't it apply identically to the other two eclipse WINs? The answer, per the 2026-04-13 revert reason, is that it does NOT apply — so removing it from WIN-068's evidence block is the internally consistent move.

citations_unchecked: Kim & Chang 2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054 (volume and page numbers plausible; exact DOI not verified in this review)
Chapman 1933, Terrestrial Magnetism and Atmospheric Electricity 38:3 (standard historical citation; plausible)
Yamazaki & Maute 2021, Frontiers in Astronomy and Space Sciences 8:766327 (URL present in detail_evidence; not retrieved)
Meza et al. 2021 (referenced in detail_verdict_text without volume/page; needs fuller citation)

advocate_mode.best_defense: A thoughtful dome defender reads the WIN-068 panel and says: 'Your own Section 1.8 argues the refraction cascade breaks dome eclipse predictions — that's a self-contradiction argument. Your verdict says Std Model Explains. Which is it? The text asserts both — and the stronger one (self-contradiction) is listed as a fallback, as if you lack confidence in it. Pick the strongest argument and commit. Hedging makes the review look like it's throwing everything at the wall. Furthermore, WIN-010 and WIN-025 — which share the same observational basis and mechanism — have no cascade argument at all. Why does only WIN-068 carry it? Either the reviewers haven't synced, or the cascade was added and forgotten when the verdict flipped. In either case, the inconsistency itself is a quality-control failure.' This defense lands because it's TRUE: the hedge is real and the cross-WIN asymmetry is visible.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Apply hole 1 and hole 2 fixes together in a single patch: subordinate the cascade to a one-line cross-reference in all three eclipse WINs (010, 025, 068), pointing to Section 1.8. Retain the Chapman/triple-counting argument as the primary evidence in each. That makes the three WINs internally consistent AND eliminates the hedge attack surface.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: WIN-010 (BOU 2017) and WIN-025 (2024 9-station) share the same dome mechanism, the same standard-physics rebuttal (Chapman + Sq), and the same verdict (Std Model Explains) as WIN-068 — but do NOT include the refraction cascade paragraph. WIN-068 alone carries it. If the cascade argument is valid for the ensemble, it is valid for the single-station and 9-station subsets as well; if it is invalid (per the 2026-04-13 revert rationale), it should not appear in WIN-068 either. WIN-012 (magnetic-gravity coupling constant = 10.9/6.5 = 1.67 nT/μGal) depends on the BOU 2017 number that WIN-010 treats as standard-model-explained; current review is consistent on that front (no new inconsistency with WIN-012).

claims_checked: H(r) = 8537·exp(-r/8619) → produces H=3,618 km at r≈7,402 km and H=1,975 km at r≈12,618 km
quantitative_verification.computations: Ran: r_TC = -8619*ln(3618/8537) = 7,402 km and r_TCap = -8619*ln(1975/8537) = 12,618 km via node math. Confirmed FSF range ratio 2.075/0.642 ≈ 3.23. Year span 2016−1991 = 25 ✓. Did NOT verify the dome's r↔latitude mapping (that is Section 1.8's job) — see hole 5.
